main_mechanisms: The forecast is anchored on the BEA 2025:Q4 real GDP level of 143581.6, with recovery from the recent year-over-year decline but continued exposure to federal-sector shocks.

A large McDuffie win would likely strengthen the mandate for public safety, government accountability, and economic development priorities that could modestly improve business confidence and private investment.
Child care and affordability policies could support labor-force attachment, but the GDP effect is likely gradual and smaller than federal spending and national-cycle effects.
The 20-point margin is interpreted as a mandate and political-environment signal, so the central forecast is modestly above a close McDuffie outcome.
uncertainty_notes: Federal employment, procurement, and national macro conditions dominate D.C. real GDP uncertainty through 2030.
The latest BEA baseline shows a negative year-over-year reading, making the speed of normalization uncertain.
Local mayoral policy affects measured chained-dollar GDP with implementation lags and partial control over land use, public safety, and business conditions.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is not a close-election RD cell; the 20-point McDuffie margin is mainly a mandate and broader political-environment contrast, not just the causal effect of barely electing McDuffie.

main_mechanisms: The BEA 2025:Q4 level of 143581.6 is the anchor, with the median assuming a partial rebound from the recent decline and moderate real growth by 2030:Q4.
A 10-point McDuffie win would plausibly support economic development, public safety, and accountability policies that modestly improve private-sector activity and commercial confidence.
Affordability and child care priorities may ease labor supply constraints, but their effect on aggregate real GDP is likely incremental.
The mandate effect is positive but smaller than in the 20-point McDuffie scenario.
uncertainty_notes: Federal budget and procurement changes could move D.C. output more than mayoral policy choices.
The recent BEA series is volatile, including a negative year-over-year baseline, so the recovery path is uncertain.
Policy implementation depends on Council cooperation and agency capacity.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is mostly a mandate and political-environment scenario rather than a clean near-cutoff RD contrast; the 10-point margin says more about governing conditions than the effect of barely electing McDuffie.

main_mechanisms: The BEA 2025:Q4 level of 143581.6 anchors the forecast, with the median reflecting moderate recovery rather than a new invented baseline.
A narrow McDuffie win implies his public safety, economic development, accountability, affordability, and child care agenda is implemented with limited mandate strength.
Business-confidence effects are positive but modest because a one-point election would likely constrain political capital and require Council bargaining.
Federal-sector and national-cycle forces remain the main drivers of the 2030:Q4 real GDP level.
uncertainty_notes: A close result increases uncertainty about implementation durability and legislative cooperation.
Real GDP revisions and chained-dollar measurement can change the apparent level by 2030.
Federal government activity, office demand, and regional labor-market conditions may swamp the mayoral treatment effect.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is the McDuffie side of the close-election RD contrast; compared with the George-wins-by-1 cell, it is the best cell here for isolating the effect of barely electing McDuffie from broader mandate effects.

main_mechanisms: The forecast uses the BEA 2025:Q4 level of 143581.6 as the anchor and assumes moderate real growth by 2030:Q4.
A narrow George win would likely advance affordability, tenant protections, public services, safe streets, transit, bike lanes, and housing-production goals, but with limited mandate strength.

The larger housing-production emphasis could support construction and labor-market access over time, while tenant protections and regulatory uncertainty could offset some private real-estate investment.
Because the win is narrow, the central GDP effect is close to the McDuffie close-win scenario and dominated by federal and national conditions.
uncertainty_notes: A one-point result implies high political uncertainty and dependence on Council bargaining.
The net GDP effect of housing production, tenant protections, transit, and public-service changes is directionally mixed over a five-year horizon.
Federal spending and employment are the largest uncertainty for D.C. real GDP.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is the George side of the close-election RD contrast; compared with the McDuffie-wins-by-1 cell, it is the best cell here for isolating the effect of barely electing George from broader mandate effects.

main_mechanisms: The BEA 2025:Q4 real GDP level of 143581.6 is the measurement anchor, with the median assuming recovery from the recent decline and moderate growth through 2030.
A 10-point George win would likely give more room to pursue housing production, public services, transit, safe streets, and affordability measures than a one-point win.

Housing and transportation investments could lift construction, population capacity, and labor-market access, while stronger tenant protections could modestly reduce some private investment incentives.
The positive mandate effect from a larger George margin is offset by uncertainty about business response and implementation lags.
uncertainty_notes: The mix of housing supply expansion and tenant-protection policy has ambiguous measured-GDP effects by 2030:Q4.
Federal-sector shocks and the national cycle remain larger than the expected mayoral policy effect.
Implementation depends on budget capacity, agency delivery, and Council alignment.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is not a clean near-cutoff RD cell; the 10-point George margin mainly represents a mandate and political-environment contrast, with only limited information about the effect of barely electing George.

main_mechanisms: The distribution is anchored to the BEA 2025:Q4 real GDP level of 143581.6 and projects a moderate five-year recovery by 2030:Q4.
A 20-point George win would create the strongest mandate in this grid for housing production, public services, transit, safe streets, bike lanes, and affordability priorities.

Expanded housing supply and transportation improvements could raise construction activity, population capacity, and labor access, but tenant protections and regulatory changes could create offsetting private-investment drag.

The large margin is treated as a political-environment signal, so the central forecast is above the close George outcome but still close to the McDuffie scenarios because macro and federal forces dominate.
uncertainty_notes: The scale, timing, and financing of housing and transit-related changes are uncertain.
Federal government activity and national economic conditions could push the 2030 level far outside the policy-effect range.
BEA revisions and chained-dollar sector weights may alter measured real GDP even if local activity follows the expected path.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is a mandate and political-environment scenario, not a near-cutoff RD contrast; the 20-point George margin captures broader political support for her agenda as much as candidate selection itself.

main_mechanisms: Forecast interprets the target as route miles, anchored to the DDOT/DC Open Data stock of 108.51 route miles; 169.37 lane-miles is supporting context only.

A large McDuffie win points to a governing coalition less centered on bike-lane expansion than George's safe-streets platform, but existing DDOT projects and normal resurfacing opportunities still add route miles.
Public safety, affordability, economic development, and government accountability priorities compete for budget and staff attention, moderating bike-lane growth through 2030.
A decisive margin could reduce pressure to bargain with bike and transit advocates, lowering expected additions relative to close or George-win scenarios.
uncertainty_notes: The DC Open Data layer is a current stock snapshot rather than a historical buildout series, so the annual pace is inferred from policy context rather than a prompt-provided trend.
Council support, DDOT delivery capacity, litigation, curb-use conflicts, and federal funding conditions could move the 2030 stock materially.
Quantiles allow for low-growth outcomes with little net expansion and high-growth outcomes where protected-lane projects accelerate.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This 20-point McDuffie win is mainly a mandate and political-environment scenario, not the close-election RD contrast. It should not be compared to a George landslide as the near-cutoff candidate effect.

main_mechanisms: Forecast interprets the target as route miles, anchored to the DDOT/DC Open Data stock of 108.51 route miles; 169.37 lane-miles is supporting context only.
A 10-point McDuffie win still implies a McDuffie administration with less explicit bike-lane emphasis than George, while leaving room for continuation of already planned DDOT network projects.
The smaller margin than a landslide suggests somewhat more responsiveness to safe-streets constituencies and Council pressure.
Budget, permitting, and curb-space tradeoffs keep the median increase moderate rather than transformative.
uncertainty_notes: The prompt provides a stock baseline, not a time series, so uncertainty about the pre-2027 construction pace is substantial.
Some bike-lane projects can proceed administratively, while politically contested corridors can stall for years.
The range reflects both possible continuity with current DDOT practice and possible deprioritization under competing mayoral priorities.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This 10-point McDuffie win mostly mixes candidate control with mandate and electorate-composition effects. It is less informative for the close-election RD contrast than the one-point McDuffie scenario.

main_mechanisms: Forecast interprets the target as route miles, anchored to the DDOT/DC Open Data stock of 108.51 route miles; 169.37 lane-miles is supporting context only.
A razor-thin McDuffie win gives McDuffie agenda control but weakens any anti-bike-lane mandate, making continuation of DDOT pipeline projects likely.
George's near-win would signal demand for safe streets, transit, and bike lanes, pushing the administration and Council toward some accommodation.
McDuffie's platform emphasis on public safety, affordability, economic development, child care, and accountability still makes bike-lane expansion less central than under George.
uncertainty_notes: Close-election bargaining could matter more than the campaign platform if Council committees or agencies hold the practical levers.
The DDOT layer measures stock at a point in time; additions, reclassifications, or removals by 2030 could affect comparability.
High-end outcomes require sustained capital delivery and enough neighborhood acceptance for several major corridors.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This one-point McDuffie win is the McDuffie side of the close-election RD contrast. Compare it with the one-point George win to isolate the near-cutoff candidate discontinuity, not a mandate effect.

main_mechanisms: Forecast interprets the target as route miles, anchored to the DDOT/DC Open Data stock of 108.51 route miles; 169.37 lane-miles is supporting context only.
George's platform explicitly emphasizes safe streets, transit, and bike lanes, raising expected DDOT priority and capital attention even in a narrow win.
A one-point win limits mandate effects, so the forecast assumes acceleration over McDuffie but not unlimited ability to override neighborhood and Council constraints.
Bike-lane delivery can be bundled with street safety, bus, and resurfacing projects, increasing achievable route-mile stock by 2030.
uncertainty_notes: A narrow George win could produce either coalition bargaining that helps bike lanes or backlash that slows contested corridors.
The baseline is a stock layer, so the forecast does not rely on any invented historical construction trend.
Agency staffing, procurement timing, federal dollars, and lawsuits are large sources of uncertainty.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This one-point George win is the George side of the close-election RD contrast. Compare it with the one-point McDuffie win to estimate the candidate discontinuity at the cutoff, with minimal mandate interpretation.

main_mechanisms: Forecast interprets the target as route miles, anchored to the DDOT/DC Open Data stock of 108.51 route miles; 169.37 lane-miles is supporting context only.
A 10-point George win combines candidate control with a clearer public mandate for safe streets, transit, and bike lanes than a close win.
The administration would likely prioritize DDOT capital planning, protected-lane corridors, and street redesigns more strongly than McDuffie scenarios.
Even with a mandate, implementation remains bounded by corridor-level opposition, construction capacity, and competing transportation demands.
uncertainty_notes: The difference between route miles and lane-miles matters; these quantiles use the 108.51 route-mile anchor supplied in the prompt baseline.
Council appropriations and DDOT project management could either amplify or mute the platform signal.
A broad win may reflect a more pro-safe-streets electorate, but it could also coincide with unrelated citywide conditions that affect capital budgets.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This 10-point George win is useful for mandate and political-environment gradients among George victories. It is not the clean close-election RD contrast, which comes from the one-point scenarios.

main_mechanisms: Forecast interprets the target as route miles, anchored to the DDOT/DC Open Data stock of 108.51 route miles; 169.37 lane-miles is supporting context only.
A 20-point George win implies both a mayor with an explicit bike-lane and safe-streets platform and a strong mandate to prioritize street redesign.
Such a margin would likely improve Council and agency willingness to fund and deliver connected protected-bike-lane projects by 2030.
The forecast still assumes physical delivery constraints, procurement timelines, and corridor opposition prevent a simple doubling of the route-mile network in the median case.
uncertainty_notes: The supplied baseline is a current DDOT/DC Open Data stock measurement, not a project pipeline, so the high end is judgmental and not a new baseline.
A landslide could accelerate policy, but future budgets, DDOT leadership, and federal grant conditions may dominate the margin signal.
If the city shifts from painted lanes to protected lanes, route-mile additions could be slower but higher quality than the stock metric alone shows.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This 20-point George win primarily reflects a strong mandate and favorable political environment for bike-lane expansion. It should be kept separate from the near-cutoff RD effect.

main_mechanisms: The 2019-2025 government baseline averages 38 fatalities per year, with high volatility and a sharp drop in 2025 after 49-50 fatalities in 2023-2024.

McDuffie's platform emphasizes public safety and government accountability, which could support enforcement and agency performance but is less explicitly centered on street redesign, bike lanes, or transit priority.
A 20-point McDuffie win implies a mandate and political environment favoring his broader public-safety agenda rather than George's safe-streets and transit agenda.
uncertainty_notes: Traffic fatalities are low-count events, so year-to-year randomness remains large even if policy direction changes.
The 2025 drop and 2026 year-to-date count give mixed signals about whether the city is entering a lower-fatality regime.
The forecast does not add baseline data beyond the provided DDOT/DC Open Data history and 2026 year-to-date anchor.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This wide McDuffie-win scenario is mainly a mandate and political-environment contrast, not the close-election RD contrast; the margin suggests stronger backing for McDuffie's governing coalition and priorities.

main_mechanisms: The DDOT/DC Open Data anchor shows fatalities ranging from 22 to 50 during 2019-2025, so the central forecast remains near the recent multi-year baseline rather than extrapolating only the 2025 low.

McDuffie's public-safety and accountability emphasis could improve enforcement and implementation capacity, but his stated platform provides less direct evidence of aggressive safe-streets infrastructure changes.
A 10-point win would give McDuffie meaningful governing authority while still leaving Council, agency capacity, funding, and public acceptance as constraints.
uncertainty_notes: Fatal crash counts can move substantially from a few severe incidents, widening the predictive interval.
Policy effects by 2030 depend on capital project delivery, enforcement practice, vehicle trends, and regional travel behavior.
The 2026 year-to-date baseline is used only as an anchor and is not treated as a complete calendar-year observation.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is partly informative about candidate differences but still mostly a mandate and political-environment scenario; it is less clean for the close-election RD contrast than the 1-point McDuffie case.

main_mechanisms: The baseline has a recent high plateau in 2023-2024, a low 2025, and 14 fatalities year-to-date in 2026, supporting a central 2030 forecast in the low-to-mid 30s under a narrow McDuffie win.
A close McDuffie win isolates the candidate-officeholding contrast more than a broad mandate effect.

McDuffie's public-safety and accountability priorities could help enforcement and agency management, but the platform evidence is weaker for protected bike lanes, transit priority, and street redesign than George's.
uncertainty_notes: The close result implies limited electoral mandate, so policy differences may be muted by Council politics and implementation constraints.
Small fatality counts create wide intervals around any central estimate.
The forecast is anchored only to the provided crash-fatality series and 2026 year-to-date count.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is the McDuffie side of the close-election RD contrast: compared with a George win by 1 point, it is best interpreted as the causal effect of narrowly electing McDuffie in an otherwise similar political environment.

main_mechanisms: The DDOT/DC Open Data baseline supports a wide distribution, with recent years spanning 22 to 50 fatalities and 14 fatalities year-to-date in 2026.
George's platform explicitly emphasizes safe streets, transit, and bike lanes, which plausibly lowers 2030 fatalities through street redesign, mode shift, and protection for pedestrians and cyclists.
A 1-point George win implies little mandate, so expected reductions are modest relative to the baseline and constrained by implementation capacity and Council dynamics.
uncertainty_notes: Infrastructure and enforcement changes may take several budget cycles to affect 2030 fatalities.
Fatality totals are noisy low-count outcomes, so the interval remains broad even with a safer-streets policy direction.
The forecast does not substitute outside baseline data for the provided DDOT/DC Open Data series.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is the George side of the close-election RD contrast: compared with a McDuffie win by 1 point, it is best interpreted as the effect of narrowly electing George with minimal mandate differences.

main_mechanisms: The provided crash-fatality history anchors the forecast near recent observed levels while allowing for improvement from the 2019-2025 average of 38 fatalities.
George's safe-streets, transit, and bike-lane priorities create a plausible pathway to lower deaths by 2030 through protected facilities, traffic calming, and reduced car exposure.
A 10-point win would likely increase George's leverage over budgets, appointments, and agency priorities relative to a 1-point win.
uncertainty_notes: The size of any fatality reduction depends on project delivery, enforcement, public acceptance, and how travel demand changes by 2030.
Even strong policy changes may be swamped in a single year by random variation in severe crashes.
The 2026 year-to-date count is an incomplete-year anchor, not a new annual baseline.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This scenario mixes candidate effects with a moderate George mandate, so it is more useful for a mandate and political-environment contrast than for a clean close-election RD estimate.

main_mechanisms: The forecast remains anchored to the provided DDOT/DC Open Data series, including 49-50 fatalities in 2023-2024, 22 in 2025, and 14 year-to-date in 2026.
George's explicit safe-streets, transit, and bike-lane platform gives the strongest candidate-specific mechanism for reducing fatal crashes by 2030.
A 20-point George win would imply the clearest mandate for reallocating street space, prioritizing traffic calming, and sustaining agency focus on pedestrian, cyclist, and transit safety.
uncertainty_notes: A large mandate does not guarantee rapid capital delivery or full Council and agency alignment.
Vehicle size, speeding behavior, enforcement, litigation, and federal funding could materially change the 2030 fatality count.
Because fatalities are low-count events, the upper tail remains near recent high-fatality years even under this favorable safe-streets scenario.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This wide George-win scenario is mainly a mandate and political-environment contrast, not the close-election RD contrast; the margin implies unusually strong political support for George's transportation and safe-streets agenda.

unit: new privately owned housing units authorized by building permits
main_mechanisms: The 2025 Census BPS anchor is very weak at 1,591 units, so the forecast assumes recovery from a depressed permitting cycle rather than a return to 2019-2022 highs.
A large McDuffie win would likely strengthen an economic-development and permitting-accountability mandate, modestly improving private multifamily pipeline confidence by 2030.
McDuffie's platform is less centered on a large housing production target than George's, limiting the upside relative to a strong George mandate.
uncertainty_notes: D.C. permit totals are lumpy because a small number of five-plus-unit buildings account for most authorized units in high years.
Interest rates, construction costs, federal employment, office-market spillovers, and Council action could dominate mayoral differences by 2030.
The Census BPS series measures authorizations, not starts or completions, so filing timing can move units between calendar years.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is not a near-cutoff RD contrast; it mainly reflects a strong McDuffie mandate and political-environment scenario, so margin effects are interpreted as coalition strength rather than close-election causal identification.

unit: new privately owned housing units authorized by building permits
main_mechanisms: The forecast is anchored to the provided Census BPS collapse from 7,705 units in 2022 to 1,591 in 2025, with partial recovery by 2030.
A McDuffie win by 10 points would give some mandate for economic development, government accountability, and faster administrative execution, which could help permitted projects restart.
Because McDuffie's housing-specific production signal is weaker than George's, I do not assume a large policy-driven expansion beyond a cyclical rebound.
uncertainty_notes: Large multifamily filings can move the annual total by thousands of units, so the distribution is intentionally wide.
Regional demand, financing costs, construction costs, and federal labor-market shocks could matter more than mayoral policy.
The baseline is permits only; starts and completions may diverge from authorizations.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is mostly a mandate and political-environment contrast, not the clean close-election RD contrast; I treat the 10-point margin as giving McDuffie more governing room than a one-point win.

unit: new privately owned housing units authorized by building permits
main_mechanisms: A one-point McDuffie win keeps the forecast close to the depressed BPS anchor while allowing moderate recovery from 2024-2025 lows by 2030.
A narrow result would weaken mandate effects, so economic-development and permitting-accountability themes have only a modest assumed impact on the housing pipeline.
Compared with a narrow George win, the forecast gives less weight to an explicit large housing production target and more to general business-climate improvement.
uncertainty_notes: The April 2026 monthly baseline shows only 25 authorizations, but one monthly observation should not be extrapolated mechanically to 2030.
Permits depend heavily on financing conditions and a few large five-plus-unit structures.
Council legislation, zoning litigation, and agency capacity could materially change the 2030 total.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is the McDuffie side of the close-election RD contrast; I keep mandate effects small and interpret the forecast mainly as candidate control under a nearly tied race.

unit: new privately owned housing units authorized by building permits
main_mechanisms: A narrow George win is anchored to the same weak BPS baseline but assumes a somewhat stronger production push because her platform centers a larger housing target.
With only a one-point margin, mandate effects are limited, so implementation would depend heavily on Council cooperation and agency execution.
Tenant-protection emphasis could reduce some private developer enthusiasm, partially offsetting the production-target mechanism.
uncertainty_notes: The 2019-2025 permit history spans 1,591 to 7,705 units, showing that normal project timing can exceed the candidate-policy effect.
The balance between subsidy, zoning, tenant protections, and private financing is uncertain.
The measure is authorizations in Census BPS, not actual starts or completed homes.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is the George side of the close-election RD contrast; I keep mandate effects small and interpret the difference from a one-point McDuffie win as the near-cutoff candidate effect.

unit: new privately owned housing units authorized by building permits
main_mechanisms: A 10-point George win gives a clearer mandate for the housing-production elements of her affordability platform, raising the median above the close-election cases.
The forecast still starts from the Census BPS trough of 1,591 units in 2025, so it assumes recovery rather than immediate return to every high-permit year.
Tenant protections and affordability conditions could shift the mix toward subsidized or regulated projects and slow some market-rate filings.
uncertainty_notes: Annual D.C. permits are dominated by multifamily batches, so project timing can create large year-to-year swings.
Macroeconomic financing conditions, construction costs, and population demand remain major non-mayoral drivers.
Council and agency implementation could either amplify or dilute the platform's production target.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is not a near-cutoff RD contrast; it mainly reflects a George mandate and supportive political-environment scenario layered on top of the candidate mechanism.

unit: new privately owned housing units authorized by building permits
main_mechanisms: A large George win would be the strongest mandate scenario for her larger housing production target, so the forecast allows the largest policy-driven rebound by 2030.
The Census BPS baseline still constrains the forecast: 2024 and 2025 were both below 1,800 units, so a high 2030 outcome requires a rebuilt multifamily pipeline.
Affordability and tenant-protection priorities could change project economics, keeping the lower tail low even with a strong production mandate.
uncertainty_notes: A few large apartment permits could push 2030 above recent medians, while continued financing stress could keep authorizations near the 2024-2025 trough.
Federal employment, population growth, interest rates, construction costs, and office-conversion feasibility are outside the mayoral margin variable.
BPS authorizations are not starts, and the Census starts release does not provide a D.C.-level starts series.
near_cutoff_interpretation: This is not a close-election RD estimate; it is a strong George mandate and political-environment contrast, so the margin is interpreted as governing capacity and coalition support.
